title: Operating-System Landscape, Filesystems, Editions, and Lifecycle · Support Workbench
summary: A guided support bench extends operating-system selection and lifecycle register while retaining authorization, safety, baseline, privacy, accessibility, rollback, verification, documentation, and user communication. Planned artifact: Build a complete first version of operating-system selection and lifecycle register while every earlier competency remains active. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Distinguish desktop, mobile, server, hypervisor, appliance, embedded, real-time, cloud-managed, open-source, and proprietary operating-system responsibilities.
Compare common filesystem identity, case behavior, permissions, encryption, compression, journaling, snapshots, quotas, compatibility, and repair boundaries.
Map edition, architecture, hardware, management, security, virtualization, directory, remote-access, language, and licensing capabilities to requirements.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.config:
# comptia-a-plus cumulative fictional support evidence portfolio
# Browser labs perform deterministic text-contract review only. They never execute learner
# commands, inspect the host, touch devices or accounts, connect to networks, open equipment,
# handle malware, erase media, contact people, or claim certification.
workspace: support
ticket-id: fictional-ticket
user-impact: [replace with user outcome, scope, severity, and urgency]
environment: [replace with asset, version, configuration, owner, dependency, and evidence time]
safety-authority: [replace with consent, hazards, data custody, privacy, access, and stop rules]
baseline: [replace with expected behavior, known-good state, conditions, and measurement]
symptom: [replace with observed behavior, reproduction, frequency, timing, and evidence]
recent-change: [replace with timeline, relevant changes, counterevidence, and unknowns]
hypothesis: [replace with falsifiable cause, competing explanation, prediction, and confidence]
test: [replace with one safe reversible discriminating test, changed case, and stop rule]
observation: [replace with actual result, provenance, rejected inference, and next decision]
repair-rollback: [replace with least-change repair, authority, backup, trigger, and restored state]
verify: [replace with user outcome, original, changed, side-effect, restart, and recurrence checks]
prevention: [replace with prevention, monitoring, owner, review date, and residual risk]
escalation: [replace with stop threshold, destination, evidence packet, and requested decision]
documentation: [replace with plain structured status, redaction, access, handoff, and uncertainty]
transfer-boundary: authorized disposable equipment or simulator only
review:

steps.title: CompTIA A+ support Commit to the mission model · Workshop Os Role
steps.instruction:
workshop case — Windows, macOS, Linux, ChromeOS, mobile, and embedded devices need one support decision despite different filesystems, editions, and lifecycles. The workshop variation 6 changes the user outcome, device, version, hazard, recent change, symptom, access need, competing cause, and transfer limit. Competency probe: Distinguish desktop, mobile, server, hypervisor, appliance, embedded, real-time, cloud-managed, open-source, and proprietary operating-system responsibilities.

What must be established before implementation?

Evidence target: CompTIA A+ support Commit to the mission model · Workshop Os Role.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for distinguish desktop, mobile, server, hypervisor, appliance, embedded, real-time, cloud-managed, open-source, and proprietary operating-system responsibilities. For CompTIA A+ support Commit to the mission model · Workshop Os Role, keep Os Role connected to mechanically reviewable user-impact, asset, safety, baseline, symptom, hypothesis, test, observation, repair, rollback, verification, escalation, documentation, and transfer decision evidence.

content.text: Windows, macOS, Linux, ChromeOS, mobile, and embedded devices need one support decision despite different filesystems, editions, and lifecycles. The workshop variation 6 changes the user outcome, device, version, hazard, recent change, symptom, access need, competing cause, and transfer limit. Competency probe: Distinguish desktop, mobile, server, hypervisor, appliance, embedded, real-time, cloud-managed, open-source, and proprietary operating-system responsibilities.

For CompTIA A+ support Commit to the mission model · Workshop Os Role, challenge the documented misconception with changed-user, device, version, hazard, baseline, symptom, timeline, access need, hypothesis, test result, repair, restart, recurrence, escalation, and controlled equipment-transfer evidence: Operating systems differ mainly in their visual desktop theme.

options.text: State the Os Role preconditions, preserve earlier contracts, and name the trace or changed-input result that will decide success.
Operating systems differ mainly in their visual desktop theme. That claim fails the evidence boundary for Os Role.

For commit to the mission model, apply one familiar repair, omit user impact, authorization, safety, asset and version identity, baseline, recent change, competing cause, prediction, observation, rollback, changed-case verification, accessibility, privacy, escalation, and controlled transfer, then accept a missing symptom as support proof.

steps.title: CompTIA A+ support Test an acceptance boundary · Workshop Os Selection
steps.instruction:
workshop case — Windows, macOS, Linux, ChromeOS, mobile, and embedded devices need one support decision despite different filesystems, editions, and lifecycles. The workshop variation 4 changes the user outcome, device, version, hazard, recent change, symptom, access need, competing cause, and transfer limit. Competency probe: Select an operating system from application, device, accessibility, identity, security, privacy, management, support, cost, and user-transition evidence.

One precondition fails while output still looks plausible. What is the correct response?

Evidence target: CompTIA A+ support Test an acceptance boundary · Workshop Os Selection.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for select an operating system from application, device, accessibility, identity, security, privacy, management, support, cost, and user-transition evidence. For CompTIA A+ support Test an acceptance boundary · Workshop Os Selection, keep Os Selection connected to mechanically reviewable user-impact, asset, safety, baseline, symptom, hypothesis, test, observation, repair, rollback, verification, escalation, documentation, and transfer decision evidence.

content.title: Fault-isolation decision
content.text: Select evidence that identifies a cause, not a patch that only hides the Os Selection symptom.

content.label: Authority to consult after committing
content.value: CompTIA A+ Core 1 220-1201 Exam Objectives — Controls every current Core 1 mobile, networking, hardware, virtualization, cloud, and troubleshooting objective and weighting.

stimulus.title: Os Selection failure evidence
stimulus.caption: A plausible visible result conflicts with the governing contract and requires a cause-level repair.

lines.label: observed
lines.text: The technician’s most familiar operating system is the lowest-risk choice for every user.

lines.label: required
lines.text: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Select an operating system from application, device, accessibility, identity, security, privacy, management, support, cost, and user-transition evidence.

options.text: For test an acceptance boundary, apply one familiar repair, omit user impact, authorization, safety, asset and version identity, baseline, recent change, competing cause, prediction, observation, rollback, changed-case verification, accessibility, privacy, escalation, and controlled transfer, then accept a missing symptom as support proof.
Reject the unsupported conclusion, name the violated Os Selection condition, repair the responsible layer, and rerun the boundary trace.
Keep the plausible output and hide the assumption to avoid confusing the stakeholder. That claim fails the evidence boundary for Os Selection.

steps.title: CompTIA A+ support Defend the transferred model · Workshop Os Selection

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Select an operating system from application, device, accessibility, identity, security, privacy, management, support, cost, and user-transition evidence.
